nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and muddy substrate areas of Lake Malawi, often near the bottom in open water and intermediate zones between rocky and sandy habitats; found throughout the lake at various depths

decorPreferences: large open sandy substrate for ambush hunting behaviour
rocky structures along the back and sides for territory delineation
minimal planting — robust cichlid-proof plants (Vallisneria, Anubias) may be used

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild; uses tonic immobility (feigned death) on the substrate to ambush smaller fish that investigate the apparently dead body. In the aquarium, feed meaty frozen foods (silversides, lance fish, prawns, cockles, mussels, krill) and large sinking carnivore pellets. Live feeder fish are generally discouraged due to disease risk and potential to reinforce aggressive hunting behaviour toward tankmates. Should not be fed mammalian meats (beef heart) long-term. Feed 1–2 times daily.

goodWith: large robust Lake Malawi haplochromines of similar size (e.g., Copadichromis, Otopharynx, Placidochromis spp.)
large Lake Malawi peacock cichlids (Aulonocara spp.) of comparable size
large Mbuna species in very large tanks with caution
robust catfish (Synodontis spp.) as bottom dither fish
avoidWith: small fish of any species — will be ambush-hunted as prey
fish small enough to fit in its mouth
Nimbochromis venustus (Venustus) and other closely related Nimbochromis — intense conspecific aggression; natural prey species overlap
Aulonocara spp. in cramped tanks — may outcompete and prey upon them

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Nimbochromis livingstonii showing the characteristic brown blotched pattern on adult male.
Wikimedia Commons image showing adult male in aquarium displaying characteristic blotched coloration.

summary: The Livingstonii, or Livingston's Hap (Nimbochromis livingstonii), is a large Lake Malawi cichlid famous for feigning death on the substrate to ambush smaller prey. Males develop vivid iridescent blue-green coloration over dark brown blotches. Best suited to species tanks of 400 L or more alongside robust, similarly sized Malawi cichlids.
careNotes: The Livingstonii, also known as Livingston’s Hap or Kalingono, is one of the most behaviourally distinctive Lake Malawi cichlids. It hunts using a remarkable ambush strategy: the fish lies motionless on the substrate, mimicking a dead or decaying fish, then strikes at smaller fish that come to investigate. This tonic immobility (feigned-death) hunting behaviour is unique among African cichlids and should be kept firmly in mind when selecting tankmates — no fish small enough to be swallowed should be housed with this species. A minimum tank of 400 L with a footprint of at least 150 cm is required; 500 L or more is preferable for a male-female pair or a small group of one male with two females. Maintain water at the hard, alkaline parameters typical of Lake Malawi (pH 7.5–8.5, gH 10–20, temperature 24–28°C) and perform water changes of 25–30% weekly. Males become highly territorial, especially with conspecifics and other Nimbochromis; a single male is best kept with multiple females or alongside species that occupy different ecological niches. Juveniles display a distinctive mottled brown-and-white blotch pattern — sometimes sold in the trade as ‘Sleeper’ or under the local name Kalingono — and buyers should be aware that the adult male transformation is dramatic: mature males develop vivid blue-green iridescent flanks that contrast with retained dark blotches. Aggression is high — a tightly fitting lid is recommended, as stressed fish may jump. Provide rocky structures for territory delineation and a large open sand area to replicate the natural ambush habitat.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. The male courts females aggressively and spawning occurs on a flat sandy substrate. The female incubates 30–80 eggs in her buccal cavity for approximately 21–28 days. During this period the female should not be disturbed or netted, as she may swallow or drop the brood under stress. Once the fry are released, they are large enough to accept crushed cichlid pellets and baby brine shrimp. Raising fry in a separate grow-out tank is advisable, as the parents may not be reliably protective. Breeding difficulty is moderate; a single male with two or three females works well. Males may harass females persistently after spawning; separating the brooding female from the male is often recommended.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nimbochromis livingstonii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 25 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (7.5–8.5), hardness, native range (Lake Malawi), diet (piscivore), tonic immobility ambush behaviour, breeding mode (maternal mouthbrooder), and tank size recommendations.

additional.name: FishBase — Nimbochromis livingstonii
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 25.0 cm TL), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Lake Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania), depth range, and diet classification (piscivore). Confirmed lifespan and water parameter ranges.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Haplochromis livingstonii (Livingston’s Cichlid) Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide; confirmed tank size minimum (75–100 US gal / ~280–380 L floor; 400 L adopted as appropriate for a species reaching 25 cm), diet (piscivore, frozen meaty foods), temperament (aggressive), and compatibility guidance for Malawi community tanks.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Nimbochromis livingstonii species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed tonic immobility ambush predation strategy in detail, adult coloration transformation from juvenile blotched phase to iridescent adult male, and mouthbrooding breeding mode. Supported careLevel=intermediate classification.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Nimbochromis livingstonii species profile
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community profile; confirmed minimum tank dimensions (6 ft / 180 cm footprint recommended for groups), conspecific aggression, breeding parameters (clutch size 30–80, incubation 21–28 days), and habitat notes for sandy intermediate zone of Lake Malawi.

fish.breedingNotes: A maternal mouthbrooder: the female incubates 30–80 eggs in her mouth for 21–28 days. Separating the brooding female from the male during this period is generally advised, as the male may harass her persistently. Fry are large enough on release to accept crushed cichlid pellets and baby brine shrimp, and raising them in a dedicated grow-out tank is recommended.
